steps.title: Pin down the two-part claim
steps.content: The student argues (1) the mere presence of anger may not hurt perceptions of the review and (2) a high degree of anger does worsen perceptions of the review. Supporting data must therefore show low-anger reviews holding up against the control while high-anger reviews drop below it.

steps.title: Look for the matching contrast
steps.content: We need a comparison anchored to the control (no anger) on helpfulness of the review — low-anger ratings should be at least as high as the control, while high-anger ratings should be clearly lower.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'helpfulness... substantially higher than... ratings of the reviewed product regardless of which type of review': compares helpfulness with product attitude across all conditions, which says nothing about how anger level changes perception of the review. ✗
  • Choice B — 'low-anger review rated... slightly more helpful, whereas... high-anger review rated... less helpful': uses the control as the baseline and shows low anger producing no harm to the review's perceived helpfulness while high anger lowers it — exactly the student's split. ✓
  • Choice C — 'attitude toward the reviewed product was slightly worse when... low-anger review': introduces a negative effect from the low-anger condition, which contradicts the student's claim that mere anger may not hurt perceptions. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Compared with participants who saw the low-anger review, participants who saw the high-anger review': compares high anger only to low anger and skips the control, so it cannot show that low anger is harmless relative to a no-anger baseline. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
